fullText:
Private AI memory cannot live on-chain as plaintext.

But proof can.

I’m sharing my paper:

Private On-Chain Validated Skill Graph

The thesis is direct:

AI systems will increasingly accumulate reusable skills.

But if those skills become institutional memory, they need governance:

who can write them
who can update them
who can revoke them
what evidence supports them
what policy they satisfy
what future missions may rely on them

GoalOS V97.4 proposes a private, on-chain architecture for the Validated Skill Graph.

The durable unit is not a prompt, transcript, or answer.

It is a validated skill:

scoped
replayable
evidence-backed
policy-gated
versioned
rollbackable
safe to reuse

The key design law:

Private intelligence is never written as plaintext.

If it must live on-chain, it is written as ciphertext plus commitments.

Zero-knowledge proofs attest that hidden policy conditions were satisfied without revealing the private skill capsule.

Ethereum stores public authority:

Evidence Docket roots
ProofBundle roots
schema hashes
version events
authorization records
zk verification results
optional encrypted capsules

The private operational intelligence remains encrypted.

The public chain records proof commitments, not exposed secrets.

Write authority is also bounded.

Only wallets that own direct *.agent.agi.eth subnames may write, supersede, or revoke skill commitments.

That matters.

Because autonomous memory should not be editable by vibes, prompts, or anonymous outputs.

It should require verifiable agent identity, policy satisfaction, validator evidence, and Chronicle admission.

This is the shift:

from AI memory
to proof-governed memory

from private capability leakage
to encrypted skill capsules

from “trust my agent”
to “verify the commitment, policy proof, and authorization path”

A model may draft.
An agent may act.
A validator may attest.

But only Chronicle-admitted capability should influence future missions.

This paper does not claim achieved AGI, ASI, empirical SOTA, legal certification, financial advice, live settlement, or production authority.

It defines a research and implementation architecture for securing reusable AI capability with commitments, encryption, ENS authorization, zk policy proofs, and proof-gated memory.

Credit:
Vincent Boucher
President, https://t.co/9VRSyr4LkC & https://t.co/dCKMr5LQ1Q

Paper:
Private On-Chain Validated Skill Graph
https://t.co/clutIG3vnE

I’m attaching the first page because the design law is worth reading directly.

The future of AI memory is not “put everything on-chain.”

It is:

keep intelligence private,
make proof public,
let only validated capability compound.

fullText:
// Memory becomes an action space //

Great paper on long-term memory for agents.

(bookmark it)

In short, it's discusses the use of a learned policy for using memory at the right granularity.

Most memory systems still hand the model whatever a retriever selected, leaving it a passive consumer of pre-picked evidence. NapMem reframes memory as a structured action space the agent navigates on its own.

It organizes user history into a multi-granularity pyramid, raw conversations, typed memory records, topic tracks, and user profiles, linked by provenance relations and exposed as tools.

Trained with memory-tool RL, the agent chooses which granularity to inspect before answering.

NapMem stays competitive across PersonaMem-v2, LongMemEval, and LoCoMo while largely preserving general reasoning and tool-use ability.

Paper: https://t.co/TSNVf4cz1H

fullText:
Great writeup from the University of Oxford.

It's a taxonomy of LLM-based agent limitations. Good read for anyone shipping with agents.

Benchmark scores keep climbing, yet the same agent failures resurface across otherwise unrelated evaluations, hidden behind the leaderboard.

This work synthesizes 27 benchmark, taxonomy, and audit papers spanning 19 benchmarks into the first cross-cutting taxonomy of LLM-agent limitations.

Six failure clusters emerge: Tool invocation and parameter errors, planning and constraint-satisfaction failures, long-horizon degradation from context accumulation, multi-agent coordination breakdowns, safety failures under adversarial or underspecified conditions, and measurement validity problems.

Failures compound nonlinearly with task length, strong sub-task scores do not add up to end-to-end success, and adding scaffolding does not reliably improve reliability.

Paper: https://t.co/mn9CRg2guo

fullText:
AI memory is not the moat.

Validated capability is.

I’m sharing my paper:

GoalOS: The Validated Skill Graph

The core thesis:

The durable asset in an autonomous proof economy is not a prompt, a transcript, or a polished model answer.

It is a validated skill.

A skill that has survived proof.
A skill with scope.
A skill with provenance.
A skill with replay instructions.
A skill with risk boundaries.
A skill with validator coverage.
A skill with rollback conditions.

Today, most AI memory systems store text:

prompts
summaries
embeddings
traces
conversation history

That is useful.

But it is not institutional memory.

Because a persuasive output can be wrong.
A copied prompt can drift.
A stored trace can lose provenance.
A successful-looking behavior can be reused outside the conditions that made it valid.

That is how autonomous systems compound errors.

The Validated Skill Graph proposes a stricter memory law:

LLMs may draft.

Only proof-admitted skills persist.

A candidate skill can come from an LLM draft, expert intervention, AGI Job, reviewer critique, or repeated mission pattern.

But it cannot influence future missions until it passes the Chronicle Gate.

The path is explicit:

Objective
→ Mission Contract
→ Proof Debt
→ Custom AGI Jobs
→ ProofBundles
→ Evidence Docket
→ Chronicle Gate
→ Validated Skill Node
→ Validated Skill Graph
→ Future Mission Influence

This is the shift:

from memory as recall
to memory as governance

from prompt libraries
to proof-carrying capability

from “the model said this before”
to “this procedure is validated for this scope, under this evidence, with this rollback path”

That distinction matters.

The next generation of agentic AI will not only need bigger context windows.

It will need admissible memory.

Memory that knows what it is allowed to reuse.

Memory that separates candidate output from validated capability.

Memory that prevents unsupported claims from becoming institutional defaults.

The Validated Skill Graph is the compounding layer of GoalOS:

GoalOS creates proof.

The Validated Skill Graph makes proof reusable.

This is not a claim of achieved AGI, ASI, empirical SOTA, legal certification, investment advice, live wallet support, or production authorization.

It is a claim-bounded architecture for one of the hardest problems in autonomous AI work:

how to make useful machine experience compound without letting unverified output contaminate the future.

Credit:
Vincent Boucher
President, https://t.co/9VRSyr4dv4 & https://t.co/dCKMr5Lici

Paper:

The future of AI memory is not “remember everything.”

remember only what has earned the right to matter.

fullText:
The next AI economy will not be built on outputs.

It will be built on proof.

I’m sharing my paper:

GoalOS: A Unified Sovereign Proof Economy Operating System

The thesis is direct:

AI can draft.
AI can reason.
AI can propose.
AI can execute.

But only validated artifacts should shape memory, settlement, future missions, or institutional action.

GoalOS proposes an operating layer for a sovereign proof economy.

A user starts with an objective.

The system converts it into:

Mission Contract
Proof Debt
Custom AGI Jobs
ProofBundles
Evidence Docket
Chronicle Gate
Validated Skill Graph
Mainnet Handoff Packet

The core principle:

AI output is not authority.

Proof is authority.

This matters because agentic AI is moving from chat into work: research, strategy, diligence, software, markets, governance, operations.

But work requires more than generation.

It requires evidence.
It requires validation.
It requires provenance.
It requires memory discipline.
It requires settlement boundaries.
It requires reusable capability.

GoalOS separates drafting from acceptance.

LLMs can generate candidate outputs, but unsupported claims become proof debt. That proof debt is converted into custom AGI jobs. Those jobs produce ProofBundles. ProofBundles enter an Evidence Docket. Only validated results pass the Chronicle Gate and become durable memory inside the Validated Skill Graph.

That is the shift:

from AI output
to proof-gated work

from one-off answers
to reusable capability

from autonomous demos
to auditable economic infrastructure

The Custom AGI Job Factory starts empty by design.

No canned workflows.
No assumed task catalog.

Each mission creates the jobs required to retire its own proof debt.

The proof level is also flexible: exploratory work can move faster; high-stakes work can require stronger validators, stricter evidence, audits, and reproducibility.

And the mainnet boundary is explicit.

Local GoalOS prepares handoff packets.

Live posting, wallet signing, escrow, bonds, disputes, slashing, and settlement remain outside local mode.

That boundary matters.

Because sovereignty does not mean pretending everything is live.

It means knowing exactly where proof ends, where settlement begins, and what must be validated before value moves.

fullText:
Meta 让生图模型学会了"先想再画"，把 test-time compute 这套"想得久=答得好"的逻辑搬到了视觉上——它会在思维链里自我修正、写代码画准确图表、联网核对事实，而"改图能拿更高分"这件事，是模型在 RL 训练里自己悟出来的。

AI 生图卷了两年画质，Meta 今天换了个维度卷：让模型别再"一句话直接出图"，而是像 agent 一样——先在脑子里推理、自我修正、需要时写代码、联网查资料，再把图画出来。

今天上线的 Muse Image（图像）和预览版 Muse Video（视频），是 Meta 超级智能实验室（MSL）的第一批媒体生成模型。图像版已经在 Meta AI app、https://t.co/oh6EhdWtHq、Instagram Stories（仅美国）、WhatsApp（部分国家）能用了，Facebook 和视频版随后。

先把跑分说清楚，别被"Meta 出手"带节奏：

～Muse Image 在 Arena 上是文生图第 2、单图编辑第 2、多图编辑第 2（截至 7/5）；
～Muse Video 是文生视频第 3。
～都是第 2、第 3，不是第 1。 真正值得看的是它怎么做的，不是它排第几。

它最反直觉的地方：把生图当成一个 agent 流程在跑。

在一个统一的 token 预算里，文本 token 用来推理、视觉 token 用来生成——同一个模型一边想一边画。具体能力有三块：

1、自我修正：在思维链里发现问题就改——细节不对做局部编辑，大错直接整张重画，事实错了就改用工具重来。
2、会写代码：为了画准确的图表、生成真能扫的二维码而写代码执行；还能接 Muse Spark 直接产出 GIF、网页、可交互小游戏。
3、会联网搜：给知识密集、涉及时事和真实事实的 prompt 做事实核对——生图这件事第一次开始追求"画得对"，而不只是"画得好看"。

而这里有个我觉得最耐人寻味的细节：
它的"自我修正"不是工程师设计进去的，是 RL 训练里自己长出来的——因为"改一版图"能拿到更高 reward，模型就自发学会了改图。跟昨天聊的 Claude 局部空间一样，又是一个"能力从奖励里涌现"的例子。

由此带来一个分水岭级的变化：生图开始"想得越久、画得越好"。

Meta 说它随推理时间投入近似 log-linear 提升——而且不是靠"生成 N 张挑最好"（best-of-N 很快就饱和），而是靠认真推理在持续 scale。这正是它和纯扩散模型的根本区别：扩散模型是一步到位，Muse 把 test-time compute 这套"想得久=答得好"的逻辑搬到了像素上。

安全这块也补了：Content Seal 隐形水印，抗裁剪、压缩、缩放甚至截图，https://t.co/D794mpIHw0 能检测，之后扩到视频。

别人还在卷"一步出图有多好看"，Meta 开始卷"出图之前它想了多少"——生图的战场，正在从画质挪到过程。

user.name: Aaron Levie
user.description: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

fullText:
Just coming off of meetings with a couple dozen enterprise IT leaders discussing AI agents. Here are a few of the common themes that stand out:

* Lots of conversation that you have to solve an operating model challenge to get the full benefits of AI. Most companies have orgs that have always operated in siloes; but agents are most effectively when they are tied to a process, which often cuts across these siloes. So the big question is how do you start to deploy centrally managed agents that can work across organizational boundaries. Who manages these agents? How do they get deployed and adopted? 

* Data fragmentation remains a major issue for most organizations. As long as data remains highly fragmented and not in standard formats, or data is not available to the right people and agents, enterprises are dealing with issues around being able to get answers from agents that are accurate or that conform to their business practices. This cuts across both systems with structured data (product metrics or revenue figures) and unstructured data (product roadmap or customer contracts).

* Clear sense that companies need to figure out what their core data moats are going to be in the future. If everyone has access to roughly the same superintelligence from the various models, then the context that you feed the models becomes proprietary value in the future. Capturing this data and getting it into a format that agents can use becomes very important.

* Everyone is trying to figure out the right metrics to manage to for AI adoption. General consensus that tokens are not the right metric per se, and people leaning more toward business outcomes (in an ideal world). For business outcomes (like more revenue or more shipped product), though, you have to get close to each individual workflow to figure out if it was successfully transformed with AI so it’s harder to manage top down.

* Growing view that enterprises are going to live in a multi-model world. Lots of interest (though early in actual adoption) in layers that can route workloads to different models (frontside or open weights) for cost or performance reasons. Also enterprises are trying to figure out what things do you give to the models directly vs. what do you separate as horizontal systems and context so you can swap any system in and out. 

* Talent for driving AI adoption and implementation still remains a major issue and topic. Many view it as something you necessarily have to train for internally due to a shortage of talent being trained on this in the outside. As an aside, this feels like it remains a huge opportunity for those that get very good at deploying and management agents in an enterprise since most companies are looking for these skills.

* The best use-cases for AI tend to be those that fundamentally change the work being done instead of just replacing an existing process and doing it more efficiently. Companies are working through their versions of this individually because it’s different per industry, but this often remains both the most exciting and higher upside uses of AI.

Many more topics discussed recently, but overall it’s clear that there’s a ton of change going on with much more to come.

Here is my review:

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗲𝘁 𝗢𝗳 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗹𝗴𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲

This massive book is incredible, with its comprehensive coverage of numerous fields of mathematics and their intersection with the world of AI, data science, and machine learning (AI+DSML). I remember the very first time that I encountered machine learning. This was 20+ years ago, and that was already after 20+ years of being drenched in advanced mathematics as an astrophysicist. 

That first encounter of mine with ML was this definition: "Machine learning is the set of mathematical algorithms that learn from experience" (slightly paraphrased from the original quote by Tom Mitchell, CMU). That definition surprised me, confused me, motivated me, and changed the course of my career from astrophysics into AI+DSML. 

This book by Tivadar Danka captures the full meaning of that definition. The book covers thoroughly the many areas and domains of mathematics through which patterns in data are detected, described, learned, and recognized - all for the benefit of powering ML and AI algorithms, applications, and aspirations. 

This book will motivate you, surprise you, and inspire you in many ways, no matter what level of mathematics has (or has not) already propelled your career journey. There is room for all of us to grow. 

This is a great book, worthy to sit on everyone's desktop, ready to help you explore and exploit the full set of mathematical algorithms that learn from experience. 

The book is accompanied by a rich GitHub code repository of Jupyter notebooks. Learn by doing! Do by learning!

Disclosure: the publisher provided me with a free review copy of the book.

昨晚还在跟圈内朋友聊，deepseek未来最好的选择是搞芯片，原因是glm之类的模型的数据飞轮开始在形成了，这个数据飞轮一旦形成，除非是模型迭代方向和破坏式创新，否则很难被超越。而ds更擅长做芯片和infra层的技术创新，这个能力做芯片最合适了。

在2025年12月份之前国产模型虽然在追赶，但都无法形成数据飞轮，大都是基于非真实数据的或低质量数据的训练，这也是为啥国产模型在26年之前一直用不起来的原因，过去的3年也是弥补国产模型走捷径，不做真实数据的收集和标注需要填的坑，直到25年12月份这个坑算真正的填上了。

从2026年开始，国产模型才真正开始被用起来，实现了真正的0-1：标志性事件就是glm4.5和minimax2.5这些模型的发布。这就使得高质量场景的数据开始被国产模型厂商收集到，这样就进入了真正的数据飞轮正循环。

随着国产模型的真实用户数据和高质量数据越来越好，一开始用2023-2025积累的真实数据用claude之类的做下蒸馏形成第一个版本。虽然国产模型第一个版本可能蒸馏了anthropic、openai等模型，但后期其实就不再主要依赖蒸馏这些模型了，基本上会进入一个新的阶段。

最后，虽然中国模型距离国外模型的能力绝对值有所拉大，但能力相对值是缩小的，这使得中国模型没有直接断代式落后。

fullText:
𝙈𝙮 𝙧𝙚𝙫𝙞𝙚𝙬:

I am always excited by the growing and highly visible relevance, appearance, and application of classical mathematical algorithms (particularly those related to learning and optimization) in practical real-world use cases. I have always loved mathematics and I easily recognized its practical applications (even in early education when my classmates thought it was boring and irrelevant). RL is one of those areas where the classical methods and theories (including game theory) are becoming both significant and critical, particularly (as in this book) in ubiquitous applications like Large Language Models, Generative AI, and AI Agents. The essential element of human feedback (in RLHF) further underscores the critical importance and value of these methods and algorithms. 

This book is an outstanding guide through these learning systems, applications, environments, policies, rewards, agents, and value propositions. Learn about RLHF, Q-learning, DPO (Direct Preference Optimization), PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization), RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback), and more.

I highly recommend this book for all readers - not only for developers and deployers of LLMs and AI Agents, but for everyone who will eventually (if not already) play a role in giving feedback to AI applications emerging in nearly all daily activities.

Disclosure: the publisher provided me with a free review copy of the book.

fullText:
Linear Algebra and Optimization for Machine Learning [516-page textbook]: https://t.co/s6oCEvVMKS
—
This textbook is organized around its 2 main themes:
[Amazon Summary]

1. Linear algebra and its applications: The chapters focus on the basics of linear algebra together with their common applications to singular value decomposition, matrix factorization, similarity matrices (kernel methods), and graph analysis. Numerous machine learning applications have been used as examples, such as spectral clustering, kernel-based classification, and outlier detection. The tight integration of linear algebra methods with examples from machine learning differentiates this book from generic volumes on linear algebra. The focus is clearly on the most relevant aspects of linear algebra for machine learning and to teach readers how to apply these concepts.

2. Optimization and its applications: Much of machine learning is posed as an optimization problem in which we try to maximize the accuracy of regression and classification models. The “parent problem” of optimization-centric machine learning is least-squares regression. Interestingly, this problem arises in both linear algebra and optimization, and is one of the key connecting problems of the two fields. Least-squares regression is also the starting point for support vector machines, logistic regression, and recommender systems. Furthermore, the methods for dimensionality reduction and matrix factorization also require the development of optimization methods. A general view of optimization in computational graphs is discussed together with its applications to back propagation in neural networks.

RLHF is a powerful approach to AI alignment and human-centered machine learning. By combining reinforcement learning algorithms with human feedback signals, RLHF has become a key method for improving the safety, reliability, and alignment of large language models (LLMs).

This book begins with the foundations of reinforcement learning and policy optimization, including algorithms such as proximal policy optimization (PPO), and explains how reward models and human preference learning help fine-tune AI systems and generative AI models.
